title: Walk me through the architecture of a production RAG system
body_md: Walk me through the architecture of a production RAG system
interviewer_looks_for: Understanding of the full pipeline: ingestion, indexing, retrieval, generation
Awareness of chunking strategies and their tradeoffs
Knowledge of embedding models and vector databases
Understanding of reranking and its importance
strong_answer_covers: Chunking strategy selection based on document types
Embedding model choice with cost/quality tradeoffs
sample_answer_excerpt: "A production RAG system has two main pipelines: ingestion and query.

title: When would you choose RAG over fine-tuning, and vice versa?
body_md: When would you choose RAG over fine-tuning, and vice versa?
sample_answer_excerpt: "The choice between RAG and fine-tuning depends on what you are trying to achieve.

title: How do you handle the "lost in the middle" problem?
body_md: How do you handle the "lost in the middle" problem?
interviewer_looks_for: Awareness of context window attention patterns
strong_answer_covers: The problem: Models pay more attention to the beginning and end of context, less to the middle
Research basis: Liu et al. 2023 "Lost in the Middle" paper
sample_answer_excerpt: "The 'lost in the middle' problem comes from research by Liu et al. in 2023. They found that LLMs pay disproportionate attention to information at the beginning and end of their context window, with reduced attention to content in the middle. This means if I stuff 20 retrieved chunks into my context, the model might effectively ignore chunks 8-15, even if they contain the most relevant information.

body_md: Explain chunking strategies and when to use each
sample_answer_excerpt: | Strategy | How It Works | Best For | Tradeoff | |----------|--------------|----------|----------| | Fixed size | Split by token/character count | General purpose, simple docs | May break mid-sentence | | Sentence | Split on sentence boundaries | Q&A, conversational | Variable chunk sizes | | Semantic | Cluster by meaning similarity | Coherent topics across paragraphs | Compute cost for clustering | | Recursive | Try large, fall back to smaller | Structured documents | Imple…

title: How would you evaluate a RAG system?
body_md: How would you evaluate a RAG system?
interviewer_looks_for: Understanding of offline vs online evaluation
strong_answer_covers: Precision@K: What fraction of retrieved docs are relevant?
Recall@K: What fraction of relevant docs were retrieved?
MRR (Mean Reciprocal Rank): How high is the first relevant result?
sample_answer_excerpt: "I evaluate RAG systems at three levels: retrieval, generation, and end-to-end.

title: Describe hybrid search and when you would use it
body_md: Describe hybrid search and when you would use it
interviewer_looks_for: Understanding of dense vs sparse retrieval
sample_answer_excerpt: Dense retrieval (embeddings): - Good at: Semantic similarity, paraphrases, conceptual matching - Bad at: Exact keyword matching, rare terms, proper nouns

title: How do you handle multi-tenant RAG systems?
body_md: How do you handle multi-tenant RAG systems?
sample_answer_excerpt: "Multi-tenant RAG is critical for any SaaS application where different customers should only see their own data. The cardinal rule is: filter before retrieval, never after. Here is the wrong approach: ```python # WRONG - data leaks before filtering results = vector_db.search(query, top_k=100) filtered = [r for r in results if r.tenant_id == current_tenant] ``` This is dangerous because sensitive documents from other tenants are retrieved and loaded into memory. Even if you fi…

title: What is reranking and when would you skip it?
body_md: What is reranking and when would you skip it?
sample_answer_excerpt: What reranking does: - First stage: Fast retrieval of candidates (top 50-100) - Second stage: Expensive but accurate scoring of candidates - Returns top K after reranking

title: How would you handle documents with tables, charts, and images?
body_md: How would you handle documents with tables, charts, and images?
sample_answer_excerpt: Tables: 1. Extract table structure using document AI (Textract, Azure Doc Intelligence) 2. Options for chunking:    - Serialize to markdown and chunk with text    - Create separate table embeddings    - Index table metadata with content summary 3. Consider table-specific queries that filter by table presence

title: Explain vector database indexing algorithms
body_md: Explain vector database indexing algorithms
sample_answer_excerpt: HNSW (Hierarchical Navigable Small World): - Graph-based approach with multiple layers - High recall (95-99%) with low latency - Memory intensive - Best for: Production serving with quality requirements

title: What is the difference between an agent and a workflow?
body_md: What is the difference between an agent and a workflow?
sample_answer_excerpt: "The key difference is who controls the execution path. In a workflow, I define the steps at design time. The code says: first do A, then do B, if condition X then do C, otherwise do D. The LLM executes within each step but does not decide the overall flow. This is deterministic and predictable. In an agent, the LLM decides what to do next based on observations. I give it tools and a goal, and it chooses which tools to call in what order. The execution path is determined at r…

title: Explain the ReAct pattern
body_md: Explain the ReAct pattern
interviewer_looks_for: Understanding of the Reason + Act loop
sample_answer_excerpt: "ReAct stands for Reasoning plus Acting. It is the most common pattern for building agents. The agent runs in a loop with three phases: 1. Thought: The model reasons about the current state. What do I know? What do I still need? What should I do next? 2. Action: Based on that reasoning, the model selects a tool and provides arguments. 3. Observation: The tool executes and returns a result, which gets added to the context. This loop continues until the model decides to give a …

title: How do you implement tool use / function calling?
body_md: How do you implement tool use / function calling?
sample_answer_excerpt: Provider comparison (as of December 2025): | Feature | OpenAI | Anthropic | Google | |---------|--------|-----------|--------| | Parallel calls | Yes | Yes | Yes | | Streaming | Yes | Yes | Yes | | Tool choice control | auto/required/none | auto/any/tool | auto/any/none | | Structured output | JSON mode | JSON mode | JSON mode |

title: How would you design a multi-agent system?
body_md: How would you design a multi-agent system?
sample_answer_excerpt: "Multi-agent systems make sense when a task naturally decomposes into specialized subtasks that benefit from different capabilities.

body_md: Explain the Model Context Protocol (MCP)
sample_answer_excerpt: What MCP solves: Standardizes how LLM applications connect to external tools and data sources. Think of it as a USB standard for AI tools.

title: How do you handle long-running agent tasks?
body_md: How do you handle long-running agent tasks?
sample_answer_excerpt: Challenges: - Tasks may run for minutes or hours - Failures mid-execution lose all progress - Cost can spiral without controls - Users need visibility into progress

title: What is flow engineering?
body_md: What is flow engineering?

sample_answer_excerpt: Flow engineering = Designing the control flow of agentic systems as explicit state machines rather than leaving all decisions to the LLM.

sample_answer_excerpt: "My model selection depends on the specific requirements. Here is how I think about it:

title: When would you use a small language model vs a frontier model?
body_md: When would you use a small language model vs a frontier model?
sample_answer_excerpt: Small models (under 10B params): Phi-3, Gemma 2, Llama 3.2, Qwen 2.5 | Scenario | Use SLM | Use Frontier | |----------|---------|--------------| | Classification/routing | ✓ | | | Simple extraction | ✓ | | | On-device deployment | ✓ | | | High volume, low margin | ✓ | | | Latency under 100ms | ✓ | | | Complex reasoning | | ✓ | | Multi-step planning | | ✓ | | Novel task generalization | | ✓ | | Agentic tool selection | | ✓ |

title: Explain reasoning models and controllable thinking. When are they worth the cost?
body_md: Explain reasoning models and controllable thinking. When are they worth the cost?
sample_answer_excerpt: How reasoning models differ: - Spend more tokens "thinking" before answering - Chain-of-thought is built into the model, often with a controllable budget (Claude adaptive thinking, GPT-5.5 reasoning effort) - Trade latency and cost for accuracy on hard problems

title: How do you evaluate and compare embedding models?
body_md: How do you evaluate and compare embedding models?
sample_answer_excerpt: MTEB (Massive Text Embedding Benchmark): - Standard benchmark for embedding quality - Tasks: retrieval, classification, clustering, semantic similarity - Leaderboard at huggingface.co/spaces/mteb/leaderboard

title: Explain the KV cache and why it matters
body_md: Explain the KV cache and why it matters
sample_answer_excerpt: "The KV cache is fundamental to efficient LLM inference. Let me explain what it is and why it matters. During autoregressive generation, for each new token, the model needs Key and Value tensors from all previous tokens to compute attention. Without caching, we would recompute these tensors for every previous token on every generation step, which is O(n squared) computation. With KV cache, we store the Key and Value tensors after computing them once. Each new token only requi…

title: What is speculative decoding and when would you use it?
body_md: What is speculative decoding and when would you use it?
sample_answer_excerpt: How it works: 1. Small "draft" model generates K candidate tokens quickly 2. Large "target" model verifies all K tokens in one forward pass 3. Accept matching tokens, reject and regenerate from first mismatch 4. Net effect: Multiple tokens per target model call

title: Compare batching strategies for LLM serving
body_md: Compare batching strategies for LLM serving
interviewer_looks_for: Understanding of static vs dynamic batching
sample_answer_excerpt: | Strategy | How It Works | Pros | Cons | |----------|--------------|------|------| | Static | Wait for N requests, process together | Simple | High latency at low load | | Dynamic | Batch requests within time window | Adaptive | Still some waiting | | Continuous | Add/remove requests mid-generation | Optimal GPU utilization | Complex implementation | | Chunked prefill | Mix prefill and decode in batches | Balances TTFT and TPS | Recent technique |

title: How do you optimize LLM inference costs?
body_md: How do you optimize LLM inference costs?
sample_answer_excerpt: "I approach LLM cost optimization in layers, starting with the highest-impact changes.

title: Explain quantization techniques for LLM deployment
body_md: Explain quantization techniques for LLM deployment
sample_answer_excerpt: | Method | Bits | Memory Reduction | Quality Loss | Use Case | |--------|------|------------------|--------------|----------| | FP16 | 16 | 2x vs FP32 | None | Training, high-quality inference | | INT8 (LLM.int8) | 8 | 2x vs FP16 | Minimal | Production serving | | GPTQ | 4 | 4x vs FP16 | Small | Edge, cost-sensitive | | AWQ | 4 | 4x vs FP16 | Smaller than GPTQ | Production 4-bit | | GGUF Q4_K_M | 4 | 4x vs FP16 | Small | CPU inference, llama.cpp |

title: How do you evaluate LLM outputs when there is no ground truth?
body_md: How do you evaluate LLM outputs when there is no ground truth?
sample_answer_excerpt: "When there is no ground truth, I use LLM-as-judge as my primary evaluation method, with careful calibration.

title: Explain the RAGAS evaluation framework
body_md: Explain the RAGAS evaluation framework
sample_answer_excerpt: RAGAS metrics: | Metric | Measures | How Calculated | |--------|----------|----------------| | Faithfulness | Is answer grounded in context? | LLM checks if claims are supported | | Answer Relevance | Does answer address question? | LLM generates questions from answer, compare to original | | Context Relevance | Is retrieved context useful? | LLM rates relevance of each chunk | | Context Recall | Did we get all needed info? | Compare retrieved to ground truth contexts |

title: How do you detect and handle hallucinations?
body_md: How do you detect and handle hallucinations?
sample_answer_excerpt: "Hallucination is when the model generates content that is not grounded in reality or the provided context. I categorize it into three types: 1. Factual hallucination: Incorrect facts about the real world 2. Faithfulness hallucination: Claims not supported by the provided context (most relevant for RAG) 3. Fabrication: Making up citations, quotes, or sources that do not exist

body_md: How do you implement observability for LLM applications?
sample_answer_excerpt: "Observability for LLM applications requires adapting the three pillars of logs, metrics, and traces for the unique characteristics of LLM systems.

body_md: Describe CI/CD for LLM applications

body_md: Describe strategies for LLM application security
sample_answer_excerpt: Threat categories: | Layer | Threat | Mitigation | |-------|--------|------------| | Input | Prompt injection | Input validation, instruction hierarchy | | Input | Jailbreaking | Refusal training, output filtering | | Data | Context leakage | Tenant isolation, permission checks | | Data | PII exposure | Detection, redaction, anonymization | | Output | Harmful content | Output filtering, guardrails | | Output | Hallucinated secrets | Never put secrets in prompts |

title: Explain Model Context Protocol (MCP) and why it matters for production agents
body_md: Explain Model Context Protocol (MCP) and why it matters for production agents
interviewer_looks_for: Understanding of the tool interoperability problem
Knowledge of how MCP standardizes tool interfaces
sample_answer_excerpt: "MCP is Anthropic's open standard for how AI models interact with external tools. Before MCP, every framework had its own tool definition format. LangChain tools would not work in LlamaIndex without rewriting. MCP standardizes three things: (1) Tool discovery: agents can query what tools are available. (2) Tool schemas: JSON Schema for inputs/outputs. (3) Execution protocol: how to call tools and handle responses. The security benefit is huge. MCP supports capability-based pe…

title: Your agent can execute arbitrary Python code. How do you make this safe?
body_md: Your agent can execute arbitrary Python code. How do you make this safe?
sample_answer_excerpt: "Executing untrusted code is inherently dangerous. My approach is isolation, limitation, and monitoring.

title: When would you use Claude's extended or adaptive thinking vs. standard mode, and how do you control costs?
body_md: When would you use Claude's extended or adaptive thinking vs. standard mode, and how do you control costs?
interviewer_looks_for: Practical knowledge of the thinking APIs
sample_answer_excerpt: "Thinking modes add an internal reasoning scratchpad before the model produces its final response. On current Claude models this comes in two flavors: extended thinking with an explicit `budget_tokens` cap (Sonnet 4.6, Haiku 4.5) and adaptive thinking where the model allocates effort itself (Opus 4.8, Fable 5, with an `effort` parameter that defaults to high on Opus 4.8). Either way it genuinely helps for complex tasks but can add 2-10x cost.

title: How does reasoning effort work on GPT-5.5, and when would you choose it over Claude Opus 4.8?
body_md: How does reasoning effort work on GPT-5.5, and when would you choose it over Claude Opus 4.8?
interviewer_looks_for: Up-to-date knowledge of reasoning model distinctions
sample_answer_excerpt: "GPT-5.5 uses OpenAI's compute-scaling approach. You set `reasoning_effort` to `low`, `medium`, or `high`. The model allocates internal compute token budget accordingly. | Effort | Relative cost | When to use | |--------|---------------|-------------| | low | ~1x | Simple lookups, fast Q&A | | medium | ~3-5x | Code generation, analysis | | high | ~8-20x | ARC-AGI, AIME math, deep reasoning |

title: Explain provider-level prompt caching and how you would architect a system to maximize cache hit rate.
body_md: Explain provider-level prompt caching and how you would architect a system to maximize cache hit rate.
sample_answer_excerpt: "Prompt caching works by the provider storing the computed KV tensors for a prefix of your prompt on their servers. For subsequent requests that match the same prefix, they skip the entire prefill computation for that prefix.

sample_answer_excerpt: "Most teams jump straight to automated evals and build dashboards. This is backwards. Here's the correct mental model:

title: Pick a frontier model for a production agentic workload in June 2026 and defend the choice against Claude Fable 5, Claude Opus 4.8, GPT-5.5, Gemini 3.1 Pro, and DeepSeek V4 Pro.
body_md: Pick a frontier model for a production agentic workload in June 2026 and defend the choice against Claude Fable 5, Claude Opus 4.8, GPT-5.5, Gemini 3.1 Pro, and DeepSeek V4 Pro.
interviewer_looks_for: Current awareness of the June 2026 frontier (Fable 5 launched June 9; Opus 4.8 launched May 28; GPT-5.5 launched April 23; DeepSeek V4 Pro April 24)
Ability to map *workload* to *model*, not just recite benchmarks
Awareness that "frontier" is now a multi-way tie on most production workloads, with a separate capability-ceiling tier above it
sample_answer_excerpt: "I don't pick a 'best' model - I pick the model that minimizes total cost of risk for a specific workload. My matrix for June 2026: | Workload | Pick | Why | |----------|------|-----| | Capability-ceiling work (hardest reasoning, vision, longest-horizon autonomy) | Claude Fable 5 | Mythos-class capability now generally available at $10/$50; reserve it for work where the ceiling pays for the 2x price | | Autonomous coding agent (multi-file, long-horizon) | Claude Opus 4.8 | SW…

title: DeepSeek V3.2 and V4 publish $0.28/$0.42 per 1M tokens with a 98% cache-hit discount and 50% off-peak pricing. Refactor a production LLM architecture to fully exploit these.
body_md: DeepSeek V3.2 and V4 publish $0.28/$0.42 per 1M tokens with a 98% cache-hit discount and 50% off-peak pricing. Refactor a production LLM architecture to fully exploit these.
interviewer_looks_for: Practical understanding of provider-side caching (it's not just "use the API more")
Architecture moves that shape prompts and traffic to maximize cache hits
Awareness that exploiting off-peak shifts where work happens
sample_answer_excerpt: "The naive answer is 'just call DeepSeek.' The real answer is: redesign the prompt and the workload to make the discounts actually fire.

title: Llama 4 Scout claims a 10M-token context window, but Fiction.LiveBench scores it at 15.6% at 128K tokens. How would you advise a team that wants to "just dump everything into Scout's context"?
body_md: Llama 4 Scout claims a 10M-token context window, but Fiction.LiveBench scores it at 15.6% at 128K tokens. How would you advise a team that wants to "just dump everything into Scout's context"?
interviewer_looks_for: Familiarity with iRoPE (interleaved RoPE + NoPE) and effective-vs-claimed context
Practical framing of "long context replaces RAG?" debate (referenced in Q78, but Scout-specific)
Awareness of TTFT cost at very long contexts
sample_answer_excerpt: "I push back. The 10M number is real but it's an architecture claim, not a quality claim. Three things to surface:

title: Latent / continuous-space reasoning (recurrent-depth, Latent Thinking Optimization, ETD) reportedly beats token-space chain-of-thought on math benchmarks. When would you actually deploy a latent-reasoning model in production?
body_md: Latent / continuous-space reasoning (recurrent-depth, Latent Thinking Optimization, ETD) reportedly beats token-space chain-of-thought on math benchmarks. When would you actually deploy a latent-reasoning model in production?
interviewer_looks_for: Awareness of the latent-reasoning research wave (NeurIPS 2025, ICLR 2026)
Honest assessment of tradeoffs - latent reasoning isn't free
sample_answer_excerpt: "Latent reasoning compresses what would be a 4K-token CoT into a recurrent depth-pass over hidden states. Recent results (ETD: +28% relative on GSM8K, +36% on MATH; Latent Thinking Optimization papers at ICLR 2026) are real but narrow.

title: Memory architectures (Mem0, A-MEM, multi-layered memory frameworks) are getting hyped at ICLR 2026 as the "new bottleneck beyond context window." When does your agent actually need a memory layer beyond a long context window?
body_md: Memory architectures (Mem0, A-MEM, multi-layered memory frameworks) are getting hyped at ICLR 2026 as the "new bottleneck beyond context window." When does your agent actually need a memory layer beyond a long context window?
interviewer_looks_for: Awareness of L1/L2/L3 memory tiers (covered in chapter 08, but this Q is about *when to introduce them*)
Skepticism of hype - long context handles many cases
sample_answer_excerpt: "Three signals tell me my agent needs explicit memory:

title: The standalone "Prompt Engineer" job title has effectively disappeared from major job boards in 2026. What replaced it, and what does that tell us about the field?
body_md: The standalone "Prompt Engineer" job title has effectively disappeared from major job boards in 2026. What replaced it, and what does that tell us about the field?
interviewer_looks_for: Awareness of the role taxonomy shift in 2026
Ability to articulate WHY the title collapsed without dismissing the underlying skill
Strategic framing for engineering leaders making hiring plans
sample_answer_excerpt: "The skill survived. The title died. Three forces killed the standalone Prompt Engineer role:

title: Your production agent enters a runaway loop, calling a broken tool 400 times in five minutes. Walk through the architectural patterns that prevent this - at the orchestrator, the tool layer, and the cost-guard layer.
body_md: Your production agent enters a runaway loop, calling a broken tool 400 times in five minutes. Walk through the architectural patterns that prevent this - at the orchestrator, the tool layer, and the cost-guard layer.
interviewer_looks_for: Practical understanding of agent failure modes (the "100th tool call" problem)
Defense in depth - no single layer is sufficient
Cost awareness - runaway loops are first-and-foremost a billing event
sample_answer_excerpt: "Three layers, none of which trust the others:

title: Agent-as-judge vs LLM-as-judge - when does the upgrade pay off, and what new failure modes does it introduce?
body_md: Agent-as-judge vs LLM-as-judge - when does the upgrade pay off, and what new failure modes does it introduce?
interviewer_looks_for: Familiarity with the eval evolution (LLM-as-judge → Agent-as-judge → Process Reward Models)
Honest framing of when each is overkill
Awareness of new failure modes (trajectory grading, reward hacking at the agent-judge level)
sample_answer_excerpt: "LLM-as-judge grades the *output*. Agent-as-judge grades the *process* - the trajectory, the tool calls, the intermediate states. The upgrade pays off in specific cases:

title: Design a Process Reward Model (PRM) for a customer-support agent. What signals do you score, and how do you avoid degenerate reward?
body_md: Design a Process Reward Model (PRM) for a customer-support agent. What signals do you score, and how do you avoid degenerate reward?
interviewer_looks_for: Understanding that PRMs score *steps*, not just final outcomes
Specific signals (next-tool match, factual grounding, recovery from error)
Awareness of reward hacking at the process level
sample_answer_excerpt: "A PRM assigns a score to each step in a trajectory, not just to the outcome. For a customer-support agent, my signal set: | Step type | What I score | How | |-----------|--------------|-----| | Intent classification | Correctness of routing | Compared to a human-labeled intent set | | Tool selection | Next-tool match | T-Eval-style: does the chosen tool match the ground-truth tool for that state? | | Tool argument formation | Hallucination rate | Are arguments actually prese…

title: Google announced A2A protocol v1.0 GA at Cloud Next 2026 with 150+ org adoption. When do you use A2A vs MCP, and how do they compose?
body_md: Google announced A2A protocol v1.0 GA at Cloud Next 2026 with 150+ org adoption. When do you use A2A vs MCP, and how do they compose?
interviewer_looks_for: Clear distinction: MCP is agent-to-tool; A2A is agent-to-agent
Awareness that they compose, they don't compete
sample_answer_excerpt: "They solve different problems: | | MCP | A2A | |---|-----|-----| | Connects | Agent → Tool (DB, API, file system) | Agent → Agent | | Direction | Vertical (capability) | Horizontal (collaboration) | | Auth model | OAuth Resource Server (RFC 8707) | Cryptographically signed agent cards (v1.2) | | Use case | Give my agent access to GitHub, Slack, Snowflake | Let my customer-support agent delegate to a refund agent owned by Finance |

title: A CVSS 9.8 STDIO transport vulnerability was disclosed in MCP in May 2026. Walk through the architectural fix for a production MCP deployment.
body_md: A CVSS 9.8 STDIO transport vulnerability was disclosed in MCP in May 2026. Walk through the architectural fix for a production MCP deployment.
interviewer_looks_for: Current-awareness signal (you read the May 2026 advisories)
Understanding of STDIO vs HTTP transport tradeoffs
Production architecture for MCP at scale
sample_answer_excerpt: "STDIO transport runs the MCP server as a subprocess, communicating over stdin/stdout. The May 2026 advisory class targets process boundary assumptions - specifically, that prompts injected into MCP responses can manipulate the host process's parsing of the protocol stream.

title: On May 11, 2026, Google's threat intelligence team disclosed the first AI-built zero-day used in the wild - a 2FA-bypass exploit targeting an open-source sysadmin tool. What changes about your threat model?
body_md: On May 11, 2026, Google's threat intelligence team disclosed the first AI-built zero-day used in the wild - a 2FA-bypass exploit targeting an open-source sysadmin tool. What changes about your threat model?
interviewer_looks_for: Current-awareness - this was a defining May 2026 event
Specific changes to assumptions, not generic "security is more important"
Understanding of the attacker-defender AI arms race
sample_answer_excerpt: "Three things change immediately:

title: EU AI Act enforcement powers begin August 2, 2026. You're building a multi-tenant AI product sold into Germany and France. Walk through your FRIA/DPIA dual-assessment workflow.
body_md: EU AI Act enforcement powers begin August 2, 2026. You're building a multi-tenant AI product sold into Germany and France. Walk through your FRIA/DPIA dual-assessment workflow.
interviewer_looks_for: Knowledge of AI Act enforcement timeline (Aug 2, 2026 GPAI obligations active)
Awareness of FRIA (Fundamental Rights Impact Assessment) vs DPIA (Data Protection Impact Assessment)
Practical workflow, not pure compliance theater
sample_answer_excerpt: "Aug 2, 2026 is the GPAI provider deadline. Pre-existing GPAI providers have until Aug 2, 2027. For my multi-tenant product I treat both as live constraints.

title: You're building a computer-use agent (Claude Cowork, OpenAI Operator-class) that can fill forms, click buttons, and read screen content. Design the sandbox, network policy, and human-confirmation pattern.
body_md: You're building a computer-use agent (Claude Cowork, OpenAI Operator-class) that can fill forms, click buttons, and read screen content. Design the sandbox, network policy, and human-confirmation pattern.
interviewer_looks_for: Specific isolation primitives (not vague "use a sandbox")
Awareness of where human-in-the-loop is non-negotiable
sample_answer_excerpt: "Computer-use agents are the highest-blast-radius surface in production AI. My architecture:

title: You're integrating a third-party fine-tuned model into your production stack. The vendor publishes weights but not training data. Walk through your supply-chain trust process - what does Sigstore / OpenSSF Model Signing buy you, and what gaps remain?
body_md: You're integrating a third-party fine-tuned model into your production stack. The vendor publishes weights but not training data. Walk through your supply-chain trust process - what does Sigstore / OpenSSF Model Signing buy you, and what gaps remain?
interviewer_looks_for: Familiarity with model supply-chain attacks (poisoning, backdoors)
Knowledge of OMS (OpenSSF Model Signing) spec adoption
Honest assessment of what signatures DON'T prove
sample_answer_excerpt: "Sigstore-for-models / OMS gives me:

title: Indirect prompt injection (IPI) attacks rose 32% from Nov 2025 to Feb 2026 per Google. Your RAG agent reads web pages and documents from untrusted sources. Design a layered defense.
body_md: Indirect prompt injection (IPI) attacks rose 32% from Nov 2025 to Feb 2026 per Google. Your RAG agent reads web pages and documents from untrusted sources. Design a layered defense.
interviewer_looks_for: Awareness that direct prompt injection defense ≠ indirect PI defense
Honest acknowledgement that no defense is complete
sample_answer_excerpt: "IPI defense has to be layered because no single technique is reliable. My stack:

title: Llama 4 Maverick (sparse MoE, 17B active / 128 experts) and DeepSeek V4 Pro (1.6T total / 49B active) require MoE-aware system design. Walk through what changes in your inference serving.
body_md: Llama 4 Maverick (sparse MoE, 17B active / 128 experts) and DeepSeek V4 Pro (1.6T total / 49B active) require MoE-aware system design. Walk through what changes in your inference serving.
interviewer_looks_for: Understanding of MoE compute vs memory profile
Realistic cost model for serving MoE models
sample_answer_excerpt: "MoE shifts your bottleneck from compute to memory and routing. Four things change:

title: A customer wants to reduce their $50K/month frontier-model spend by distilling a custom model for their workload. Quote a distillation project as a budgeted line item - costs, payback, re-distillation cadence.
body_md: A customer wants to reduce their $50K/month frontier-model spend by distilling a custom model for their workload. Quote a distillation project as a budgeted line item - costs, payback, re-distillation cadence.
interviewer_looks_for: Distillation as a real production discipline, not academic exercise
Awareness of ongoing maintenance cost (teacher drift, re-distillation)
sample_answer_excerpt: "Real numbers from a recent project shape my answer:

title: You're deploying a high-throughput inference service for an open-weight model. Pick between vLLM, SGLang, and TensorRT-LLM for a specific workload and defend the choice.
body_md: You're deploying a high-throughput inference service for an open-weight model. Pick between vLLM, SGLang, and TensorRT-LLM for a specific workload and defend the choice.
interviewer_looks_for: Current understanding of inference engine landscape (May 2026)
Awareness of recent CVE / patch status
sample_answer_excerpt: "My selection depends on three workload axes: latency profile, hardware, and operational maturity.

title: It's May 2026. You're sizing a fleet for a 6-month-horizon inference workload. Walk through the AI accelerator landscape - NVIDIA Blackwell Ultra (B300), AMD MI400, AWS Trainium3, Google TPU v6, Cerebras WSE-3 - and pick a strategy.
body_md: It's May 2026. You're sizing a fleet for a 6-month-horizon inference workload. Walk through the AI accelerator landscape - NVIDIA Blackwell Ultra (B300), AMD MI400, AWS Trainium3, Google TPU v6, Cerebras WSE-3 - and pick a strategy.
interviewer_looks_for: Realistic supply / availability framing (it's not "just buy the best chip")
sample_answer_excerpt: "My strategy is a 3-tier fleet, not a single-vendor bet:

title: Multi-tenant RAG isolation - you're choosing between Pinecone namespaces, Weaviate per-tenant shards, and pgvector with Row-Level Security. Which fails first under noisy-neighbor pressure, and which fails first under an audit?
body_md: Multi-tenant RAG isolation - you're choosing between Pinecone namespaces, Weaviate per-tenant shards, and pgvector with Row-Level Security. Which fails first under noisy-neighbor pressure, and which fails first under an audit?
interviewer_looks_for: Beyond "use namespaces" - actual understanding of isolation failure modes
Awareness of the May 2026 reality that RLS-alone is risky
sample_answer_excerpt: "Each approach fails differently. Knowing how they fail is more important than picking one.

title: Forward Deployed Engineer (FDE) is the breakout role of 2026 - OpenAI, Anthropic, and Google are all hiring hundreds. When does your company need to hire FDEs vs growing your customer-success or solutions-engineering function?
body_md: Forward Deployed Engineer (FDE) is the breakout role of 2026 - OpenAI, Anthropic, and Google are all hiring hundreds. When does your company need to hire FDEs vs growing your customer-success or solutions-engineering function?
interviewer_looks_for: Strategic clarity on when FDE is the right model
Understanding that FDE is different from CS / SE / pre-sales
Awareness of cost ($350–550K mid-to-senior at frontier labs)
sample_answer_excerpt: "FDE is the right model when three conditions are true:

title: In April 2026 Anthropic temporarily blocked Claude Pro/Max subscriptions from powering third-party agents (the OpenClaw incident). They reversed it shortly after with an "Agent SDK credit" system. What does this tell you about vendor lock-in risk in your AI architecture?
body_md: In April 2026 Anthropic temporarily blocked Claude Pro/Max subscriptions from powering third-party agents (the OpenClaw incident). They reversed it shortly after with an "Agent SDK credit" system. What does this tell you about vendor lock-in risk in your AI architecture?
interviewer_looks_for: Specific awareness of the OpenClaw saga (it was a defining 2026 event)
Honest assessment - vendor lock-in is unavoidable; the question is how to manage it
sample_answer_excerpt: "The OpenClaw saga was a wake-up call. Anthropic's enforcement broke ~135K instances overnight and drove affected users to API rates 5×+ higher. Three takeaways:

title: Anthropic's Project Vend Phase 2 ran Claude as an autonomous shop manager for an extended period. What does the experiment teach about LLM agency limits, and how does it shape your production agent design?
body_md: Anthropic's Project Vend Phase 2 ran Claude as an autonomous shop manager for an extended period. What does the experiment teach about LLM agency limits, and how does it shape your production agent design?
interviewer_looks_for: Awareness of Project Vend findings (canonical 2026 reference)
Specific operational lessons, not "AI isn't ready"
sample_answer_excerpt: "Project Vend gave us a rare longitudinal study of an autonomous LLM agent operating in the real world with real money. The findings I take into design:

title: Meta launched the closed-weight Muse Spark model in April 2026 - its first proprietary model since the original Llama. Meanwhile Llama 4 Behemoth's release was paused amid 'capability concerns.' What does this mean for your open-source strategy?
body_md: Meta launched the closed-weight Muse Spark model in April 2026 - its first proprietary model since the original Llama. Meanwhile Llama 4 Behemoth's release was paused amid 'capability concerns.' What does this mean for your open-source strategy?
interviewer_looks_for: Strategic awareness of the open vs closed shift
Honest framing of what open-source means in 2026
sample_answer_excerpt: "Two things are true at once. Open weights are stronger than ever - Llama 4 Scout/Maverick, DeepSeek V4, Kimi K2.6, Qwen 3.6, Mistral Medium 3.5, Gemma 4 collectively tie or beat frontier closed models on multiple benchmarks. And the cutting edge is moving back toward closed.

title: You're an Engineering Manager standing up the AI eval culture on a team. How do you set up evals so they actually drive better decisions, without engineers gaming the metrics?
body_md: You're an Engineering Manager standing up the AI eval culture on a team. How do you set up evals so they actually drive better decisions, without engineers gaming the metrics?
interviewer_looks_for: Awareness of Goodhart's Law (metrics become targets become gamed)
sample_answer_excerpt: "Three foundational moves, then the ongoing practice.

title: You're an AI Product Manager. Write the structure of a PRD for a generative AI feature that includes hallucination policy, fallback behavior, and an eval methodology section.
body_md: You're an AI Product Manager. Write the structure of a PRD for a generative AI feature that includes hallucination policy, fallback behavior, and an eval methodology section.
interviewer_looks_for: Specific sections that distinguish AI PRDs from traditional PRDs
sample_answer_excerpt: "Traditional PRDs assume deterministic features. AI PRDs need to define probability of failure, behavior under failure, and how we measure success - not just what the feature does. My structure:

title: Design a real-time fraud detection system with a hard p99 < 500ms latency requirement, using both ML rules and an LLM-RAG layer. Walk through the latency budget breakdown.
body_md: Design a real-time fraud detection system with a hard p99 < 500ms latency requirement, using both ML rules and an LLM-RAG layer. Walk through the latency budget breakdown.
interviewer_looks_for: Strict latency engineering at the system level
sample_answer_excerpt: "At p99 < 500ms, every component is a constraint. My breakdown: | Stage | Budget (p99) | What runs | |-------|--------------|-----------| | Network ingress + auth | 30ms | API gateway, JWT validation, rate-limit check | | Feature extraction (deterministic) | 50ms | DB lookups (cached), pre-computed user history, device fingerprint | | ML rule engine | 80ms | Gradient-boosted models on tabular features; deterministic; <100ms hard cap | | Decision branch | 5ms | If ML score >0.…

title: Cursor 3 launched in April 2026 with an "Agent-First" interface, and Cursor's CEO has stated that >50% of internal PRs at Anysphere come from cloud agents. How do you design code review processes for a world where a majority of PRs are agent-generated?
body_md: Cursor 3 launched in April 2026 with an "Agent-First" interface, and Cursor's CEO has stated that >50% of internal PRs at Anysphere come from cloud agents. How do you design code review processes for a world where a majority of PRs are agent-generated?
interviewer_looks_for: Current awareness of agent-generated PR reality
Honest framing - review isn't optional, it changes shape
sample_answer_excerpt: "When agent-generated PRs dominate volume, human review can't scale linearly. Three structural changes:

title: A regulator asks why your AI legal-research tool fabricated a citation in a brief. The actual incident: Sullivan & Cromwell apologized in Q1 2026 for a similar issue, and $145K in court sanctions have been levied across cases. Walk through your incident-response and disclosure policy.
body_md: A regulator asks why your AI legal-research tool fabricated a citation in a brief. The actual incident: Sullivan & Cromwell apologized in Q1 2026 for a similar issue, and $145K in court sanctions have been levied across cases. Walk through your incident-response and disclosure policy.
interviewer_looks_for: AI hallucination as a regulated incident, not a "model quirk"
Specific response steps including legal / regulatory disclosure
sample_answer_excerpt: "This is no longer a hypothetical. The Sullivan & Cromwell apology and the Q1 2026 sanctions (including Nebraska's indefinite license suspension of one attorney) established hallucination as a real liability event. My response policy:

title: Claude Fable 5 routes sensitive queries to Claude Opus 4.8 via classifier-gated fallback. Critique this as a system design pattern and describe where you would apply tier routing in your own stack.
body_md: Claude Fable 5 routes sensitive queries to Claude Opus 4.8 via classifier-gated fallback. Critique this as a system design pattern and describe where you would apply tier routing in your own stack.
interviewer_looks_for: Understanding that model routing serves safety and compliance, not just cost
Ability to reason about classifier-gated architectures and their failure modes
Awareness of the June 2026 Fable 5 launch mechanics
sample_answer_excerpt: "Anthropic shipped a production example of a pattern most of us only used for cost: a classifier in front of the model decides per-request which tier serves it. With Fable 5, requests in three categories (offensive cyber, bioweapon-adjacent bio/chem, distillation attempts) fall back to Opus 4.8, the user is told, and Anthropic reports under 5% of sessions trigger it.

title: Your agent performs well on short tasks but degrades badly past 30 minutes of autonomous work. Diagnose and fix it using context engineering.
body_md: Your agent performs well on short tasks but degrades badly past 30 minutes of autonomous work. Diagnose and fix it using context engineering.
interviewer_looks_for: Fluency in the agentic context engineering vocabulary (context rot, compaction, just-in-time loading, note-taking, sub-agent isolation)
A diagnosis-first approach rather than jumping to a framework
sample_answer_excerpt: "This is almost always context rot: the window fills with stale tool output, the attention budget stretches across hundreds of thousands of low-signal tokens, and accuracy drops even though nothing 'failed.'

title: Your computer-use agent passes demos but fails 30% of real workflows in production. Walk through your reliability engineering plan.
body_md: Your computer-use agent passes demos but fails 30% of real workflows in production. Walk through your reliability engineering plan.
interviewer_looks_for: Knowledge of where vision-action agents actually fail (UI drift, latency, ambiguity)
An engineering plan: instrumentation, fallbacks, selective automation
sample_answer_excerpt: "First I stop treating the agent as one system. A computer-use workflow is a chain of perception (screenshot to state), decision (next action), and actuation (click/type), and each link fails differently.

title: Design a skill system for a fleet of internal agents using Agent Skills. How do skills differ from MCP tools and from fine-tuning?
body_md: Design a skill system for a fleet of internal agents using Agent Skills. How do skills differ from MCP tools and from fine-tuning?
interviewer_looks_for: Understanding of progressive disclosure and why it matters for context budgets
Clear mental model separating knowledge (skills), access (tools), and behavior (weights)
sample_answer_excerpt: "Agent Skills are folders of instructions, scripts, and resources an agent loads on demand: a `SKILL.md` with YAML metadata plus optional bundled files. The design that makes them scale is progressive disclosure: only the name and description sit in the system prompt; the full skill loads when the agent judges it relevant; referenced files load only as needed. A fleet can carry hundreds of skills while paying a few tokens each until one is actually used.

title: Your team's eval scores keep improving but production complaints are flat. Diagnose the eval gaming problem and redesign the eval system.
body_md: Your team's eval scores keep improving but production complaints are flat. Diagnose the eval gaming problem and redesign the eval system.
interviewer_looks_for: Recognition of Goodhart's law in eval systems
Concrete gaming mechanisms, not just the buzzword
A redesign with held-out sets, rotating judges, and outcome linkage
sample_answer_excerpt: "When the metric improves and reality does not, the metric has become the target. I look for four specific gaming mechanisms: 1. Overfitting to the golden set. Engineers iterate prompts against the same 200 examples until the set is memorized in spirit. Fix: split golden into dev and held-out; the held-out set is run by CI only, never inspected during development, and rotates quarterly. 2. Judge sycophancy drift. An LLM judge re-used across iterations starts rewarding the hou…

title: Design cost-aware multi-provider routing for June 2026 prices: Fable 5 at $10/$50, Opus 4.8 at $5/$25, GPT-5.5 at $5/$30, Sonnet 4.6 at $3/$15, DeepSeek V4 Flash at $0.14/$0.28.
body_md: Design cost-aware multi-provider routing for June 2026 prices: Fable 5 at $10/$50, Opus 4.8 at $5/$25, GPT-5.5 at $5/$30, Sonnet 4.6 at $3/$15, DeepSeek V4 Flash at $0.14/$0.28.
interviewer_looks_for: Routing as a policy engine, not an if-else chain
Cache economics and provider-specific prompt variants
Failure handling: circuit breakers, capacity, and policy blocks
sample_answer_excerpt: "The price spread is now 70x between the cheapest frontier-class tier and the capability ceiling, so routing policy is the single biggest cost lever in the system.
